name: Attack Surface Inventory and Exposure Mapping
description: Map all externally reachable or attacker-influenced entry points, including network listeners, file inputs, URI handlers, update channels, plugin loaders, IPC endpoints, and import pipelines. Use this as the starting point for defensive triage and hardening prioritisation.
defensive_value: Prevents blind spots by making the real review perimeter explicit before deeper analysis begins.

name: Parser and Format Boundary Census
description: Identify every parser, decoder, importer, serializer, and file-format path in the application, especially those handling untrusted or semi-trusted content. Prioritise complex formats, legacy compatibility layers, and rarely tested conversion paths for review.
defensive_value: Highlights areas where malformed content can trigger reliability or memory-safety failures.

name: Protocol and State-Machine Surface Review
description: Catalogue custom and standard protocol handlers, connection setup logic, session transitions, handshake assumptions, and message sequencing rules. Focus on places where malformed or out-of-order state can bypass intended validation or trigger inconsistent behaviour.
defensive_value: Reduces protocol confusion and unhandled-state defects that are hard to catch with simple feature tests.

name: Input Mutation and Fuzzing Candidate Prioritisation
description: Rank software components by how suitable they are for defensive robustness testing, focusing on parsers, message handlers, file import paths, RPC endpoints, and privileged local interfaces. Use this to drive safe fuzzing and negative-test coverage rather than exploit work.
defensive_value: Directs testing effort toward the most failure-prone components without requiring exploit-specific analysis.

name: IPC, RPC, and Local Service Trust Boundary Review
description: Map how the UI, local daemons, privileged helpers, background services, and extension hosts talk to each other. Verify authentication, caller validation, message integrity, and capability scoping across each boundary.
defensive_value: Exposes design flaws where local or adjacent code can gain privileges or misuse hidden service interfaces.

name: Privilege Separation and Capability Mapping
description: Document which components run with elevated privileges, which components mediate risky operations, and which code paths cross from low-trust to high-trust contexts. Verify that high-risk actions require narrow, auditable, and deliberately granted capabilities.
defensive_value: Clarifies where small design mistakes can become privilege-boundary failures.

name: Unsafe Deserialization and Object Rehydration Review
description: Review any path that reconstructs objects or executable behavior from serialized input, cached state, plugin metadata, or inter-process messages. Focus on unsafe defaults, type confusion risks, and assumptions about trusted data origins.
defensive_value: Helps identify high-impact data-handling issues without relying on exploit-chain specifics.

name: Native Bridge and Foreign Function Interface Review
description: Examine bridges between managed and native code, browser or script runtimes and native helpers, or application code and third-party SDKs. Validate argument checking, ownership rules, lifetime assumptions, and failure handling on both sides of the boundary.
defensive_value: Targets crash-prone seams where type, length, and ownership assumptions frequently diverge.

name: Updater and Package Trust Path Review
description: Map how updates are discovered, downloaded, verified, staged, and applied. Review trust decisions around manifests, package metadata, rollback handling, mirror sources, and repair or reinstall flows.
defensive_value: Protects one of the highest-trust code paths in desktop, mobile, appliance, and embedded software.

name: Code Signing and Trust Store Validation
description: Review how the application validates signed content, certificates, roots of trust, plugin signatures, and trusted publishers. Verify that failures are fatal where appropriate and that fallback logic does not silently downgrade trust.
defensive_value: Finds integrity failures that can undermine every other defensive assumption in the software.

name: Third-Party Component and SDK Dependency Mapping
description: Inventory embedded frameworks, parsers, media libraries, telemetry SDKs, browser engines, and networking stacks. Tie each dependency to its feature path and update process so risky third-party code is visible in context.
defensive_value: Improves patch readiness and clarifies where inherited risk comes from in complex software.

name: Installer, Repair, and Uninstall Workflow Review
description: Analyse installer actions, privilege prompts, service registration, file permissions, repair routines, and uninstall cleanup. These workflows often run with elevated trust and can introduce long-lived local security weaknesses if not tightly scoped.
defensive_value: Surfaces hidden security debt in setup flows that normal feature testing rarely exercises.

name: Plugin, Extension, and Scripting Engine Review
description: Map all supported and unofficial extension points, scripting runtimes, macro systems, and dynamic loading paths. Verify isolation boundaries, permission models, and what host capabilities extensions can reach by default.
defensive_value: Contains extensibility risk before it becomes an unbounded code-execution or trust-propagation problem.

name: Sandbox, Broker, and Containment Boundary Review
description: Document how the application constrains risky components such as renderers, parsers, previewers, plugins, or untrusted content handlers. Review the assumptions that separate low-trust execution from high-trust brokers and system resources.
defensive_value: Makes containment drift visible and helps prioritise hardening where untrusted content meets privileged helpers.

name: Memory-Safety Hotspot Inventory
description: Identify code regions with complex buffer handling, custom allocators, ownership transfer, concurrency-sensitive state, and manual lifetime management. Use this to focus code review, safer-language migration, and targeted robustness testing.
defensive_value: Improves the quality of defensive review around code most likely to fail under malformed or high-stress inputs.

name: Resource Exhaustion and Algorithmic Complexity Review
description: Review paths that may be vulnerable to CPU spikes, memory blowups, disk churn, or queue exhaustion under crafted but non-malicious-looking inputs. Include decompression, regex, graph traversal, parser nesting, and unbounded caching behavior.
defensive_value: Catches denial-of-service style reliability failures that can affect clients, services, and embedded devices alike.

name: Patch Diff and Security Regression Review
description: Track which files, functions, and workflows changed between vulnerable and fixed versions or across security-sensitive releases. Use the diff to confirm root-cause understanding, spot incomplete fixes, and improve test coverage without treating the diff as an exploit recipe.
defensive_value: Improves confidence that security fixes actually address the right trust boundary and do not leave near-identical regressions.

name: Crash Triage and Reproducibility Workflow
description: Standardise how failures are bucketed, reproduced, minimised, and tied back to code ownership or feature area. Focus on reproducibility, environmental assumptions, and whether a crash reflects a simple bug, a trust-boundary issue, or a broader reliability concern.
defensive_value: Turns raw crash volume into actionable defensive engineering work rather than ad hoc debugging.

name: Local Data, Secrets, and State Storage Review
description: Map where the application stores tokens, session state, caches, keys, credentials, drafts, and forensic residue. Review access control, encryption expectations, cleanup behavior, and what happens when state is copied between users or devices.
defensive_value: Reduces the chance that local state becomes an unintended high-value target or a silent cross-user leak.

name: Session, Authentication, and License Gate Review
description: Review session handling, local auth helpers, token refresh logic, cached entitlements, and license or feature gates. Ensure that security-relevant checks are not merely UI decisions and that offline or degraded modes do not silently broaden trust.
defensive_value: Clarifies whether product-state logic can unintentionally weaken security or authorization assumptions.

name: Config, Preferences, and Policy File Review
description: Examine how configuration files, local policies, preference stores, and importable profile bundles affect trust decisions, logging, network destinations, and risky feature toggles. Verify validation, ownership, and safe defaults.
defensive_value: Finds low-friction ways security posture can be weakened through seemingly ordinary configuration channels.

name: Crash Reporting, Telemetry, and Privacy Boundary Review
description: Map what error data, stack traces, attachments, and runtime context leave the device or environment during failure reporting. Validate minimization, consent, redaction, and isolation so diagnostics do not leak sensitive state or trust tokens.
defensive_value: Prevents diagnostic mechanisms from becoming data-exposure channels.

name: Logging, Alerting, and Research Evidence Preservation
description: Review how the software records noteworthy security and stability events, how logs are rotated or protected, and how analysts preserve evidence during internal research. Ensure that local and remote evidence remains useful for triage, remediation, and disclosure.
defensive_value: Supports trustworthy internal research and shortens time-to-remediation when issues are found.

name: Responsible Disclosure and Remediation Tracking
description: Tie research findings to reproducible notes, impact statements, fix tracking, retest checkpoints, and disclosure decisions. Use a consistent workflow so defensive software research leads to verified remediation instead of isolated technical notes.
defensive_value: Improves the organisational quality of software security research and reduces repeat findings.

name: Binary Triage and Component Fingerprinting
description: Identify the major binaries, libraries, packers, runtimes, and third-party engines that make up the application. Use this to separate first-party logic from inherited risk and to understand which components deserve deeper review first.
defensive_value: Speeds up defensive triage by making the software supply stack and likely risk concentration visible.

name: Symbol, String, and Feature-Flag Recovery
description: Recover meaningful names, debug remnants, feature toggles, hidden mode strings, and error identifiers that reveal the application's real operational model. These artifacts often point to unreachable code, unsupported modes, or security checks that deserve review.
defensive_value: Turns opaque binaries into reviewable components and highlights hidden behavior that product teams may have forgotten.

name: Control-Flow and Security-Decision Mapping
description: Map where the software makes trust decisions about identity, updates, permissions, unsafe inputs, or privileged actions. Focus on the code paths that actually enforce policy rather than the UI or documentation layer that merely describes it.
defensive_value: Helps validate whether the documented security design matches the real implementation path.

name: Hidden Admin, Debug, and Test Hook Discovery
description: Review the application for dormant debug menus, test endpoints, alternate launch switches, support-only features, or developer bypass hooks that may survive into production. These paths can dramatically alter trust assumptions if they remain reachable.
defensive_value: Finds high-impact shortcuts that can undermine normal authentication, authorization, or safety controls.

name: Anti-Tamper and Self-Protection Review
description: Document anti-tamper, anti-debug, integrity, and self-protection logic in the product so defenders understand what it protects, what it breaks, and where it silently fails open. The review goal is not bypass for its own sake, but trust validation and resilience planning.
defensive_value: Clarifies whether self-protection features meaningfully enforce trust or just add operational fragility.

name: Authentication State Desynchronization Review
description: Examine whether the UI, local cache, backend service, and privileged helpers maintain consistent understanding of authenticated state. Pay attention to resume flows, offline mode, token refresh, and component restarts that may leave parts of the stack out of sync.
defensive_value: Targets real-world auth failures that appear only under lifecycle edge cases rather than happy-path testing.

name: Client-Side Trust and Authorization Drift Review
description: Identify decisions that are enforced primarily in the client or presentation layer and verify that authoritative services and privileged helpers independently re-check them. Look for UI-only gates, hidden controls, and assumptions that the client will behave honestly.
defensive_value: Finds trust gaps where business logic and security policy diverge across layers.

name: Randomness, Nonce, and Identifier Quality Review
description: Review the generation and lifecycle of tokens, IDs, nonces, reset values, temporary filenames, and cache keys that carry security significance. Weak or overly predictable values can create subtle but repeatable trust failures even when the surrounding protocol appears sound.
defensive_value: Improves confidence in the application's foundational trust assumptions and replay resistance.

name: Crypto Material Lifecycle and Secret Handling Review
description: Map where keys, derived secrets, certificates, and protected blobs are created, cached, rotated, and destroyed. Review boundary conditions such as migration, backup, crash recovery, and export features where secure handling often degrades.
defensive_value: Makes secret-handling debt visible before it becomes a systemic design weakness.

name: Archive Extraction and Path Boundary Review
description: Review how the software unpacks archives, restores project bundles, imports templates, or expands compressed content into local file systems. Verify that path normalization, overwrite handling, and boundary checks behave safely across edge cases and nested containers.
defensive_value: Catches high-impact file-handling flaws in import and restore features that often look routine.

name: File Association, URI Scheme, and External Handler Review
description: Map every place the application accepts a file path, custom URI, deep link, or external handler invocation and validate the trust assumptions around it. These paths often bridge untrusted content into privileged workflows or helper processes.
defensive_value: Highlights integration points where local or remote content can unexpectedly drive sensitive behavior.

name: Template, Expression, and Embedded Script Review
description: Identify any feature that evaluates user-controlled expressions, templates, formulas, embedded scripts, or macro-like content. Review sandboxing, object exposure, and privilege assumptions around the evaluation environment.
defensive_value: Finds dangerous evaluation surfaces while keeping the review centered on safe design and validation.

name: Document Preview, Rendering, and Conversion Review
description: Review previewers, thumbnailers, renderers, and format-conversion paths that process untrusted content in the background or before a user explicitly opens it. These paths frequently have different trust assumptions and weaker isolation than the main feature flow.
defensive_value: Surfaces content-driven risk in code that product teams may not even consider part of the main application.

name: Message Grammar and Parser Tolerance Review
description: Document what malformed, ambiguous, truncated, or overlong messages the application's parsers and protocol handlers accept or reject. Focus on grammar ambiguity, inconsistent recovery, and compatibility shortcuts that create unintended behavior.
defensive_value: Improves parser robustness and helps turn vague protocol concerns into concrete engineering tasks.

name: Search Path, Load Order, and Side-Loading Review
description: Review how the application resolves libraries, plugins, helper tools, configuration overlays, and external resources at runtime. Priority order, fallback directories, and implicit trust of local paths can create systemic risk across many software classes.
defensive_value: Makes startup and dependency trust assumptions explicit before they become deployment-time surprises.

name: Localhost, Loopback, and Internal API Exposure Review
description: Map services and APIs bound to loopback or local-only assumptions, then verify which callers can actually reach them and with what data. Local APIs are frequently treated as safe by design even when browser, helper, or companion-app paths can influence them.
defensive_value: Finds overlooked trust boundaries between local software components that share a host but not a trust level.

name: WebView, Browser Engine, and Native Bridge Review
description: Review embedded browser surfaces, DOM-to-native bridges, content permissions, origin assumptions, and scheme handling. Treat these as multi-layer trust boundaries rather than simple UI widgets.
defensive_value: Exposes where rendering convenience features create hidden privilege or data-exposure paths.

name: Browser Extension, Helper App, and Companion Process Review
description: Map trust relationships between the main product and any browser extension, tray app, helper executable, or sync companion. Review how identity, authorization, and data integrity are handed off between them.
defensive_value: Reduces the risk that peripheral components quietly weaken the security model of the primary application.

name: Cross-Version Upgrade and Migration State Review
description: Analyse version migrations, schema upgrades, cache conversions, and state-import paths that run during updates or product transitions. These are high-risk moments where old assumptions and new code frequently misalign.
defensive_value: Finds security regressions created by lifecycle transitions rather than by steady-state behavior.

name: Firmware Image Unpacking and Component Inventory
description: For embedded targets, map image structure, file systems, boot components, bundled services, and update payloads without turning the exercise into exploit work. The goal is to expose hidden components, outdated libraries, and trust anchors that affect defensive testing.
defensive_value: Brings opaque appliance and firmware targets into the same structured review workflow as desktop or server software.

name: Embedded Debug, Recovery, and Maintenance Interface Review
description: Document exposed and latent maintenance paths such as serial consoles, recovery modes, vendor diagnostic interfaces, and manufacturing hooks. Focus on whether they are disabled, authenticated, or silently reachable in production deployments.
defensive_value: Highlights appliance and device trust gaps that normal application-focused testing often misses.

name: OTA, Boot Chain, and Recovery Partition Trust Review
description: Map how embedded or appliance software validates OTA updates, boot metadata, rollback policy, recovery partitions, and fallback images. These trust decisions determine whether a device stays recoverable and authentic under adverse conditions.
defensive_value: Connects device update trust to concrete recovery and integrity engineering work.

name: Crash Handler, Exception Path, and Fail-Safe Review
description: Review how the application behaves when exceptions, assertion failures, timeouts, and partial initialization states occur. Error paths often receive less scrutiny than mainline logic but may still hold elevated trust or sensitive data.
defensive_value: Improves resilience by testing what the product does when its normal assumptions break.

name: Race Condition, TOCTOU, and Reentrancy Review
description: Identify state transitions where security checks and subsequent actions are separated in time or handled across multiple threads or callbacks. Review locking, event reentry, and lifecycle assumptions around temporary files, permissions, and session state.
defensive_value: Targets subtle correctness flaws that often hide behind otherwise strong point-in-time validation.

name: Shared Memory, Cache Coherency, and Multi-Process State Review
description: Map how multiple processes or components share caches, memory segments, sockets, or local data stores. Focus on stale trust decisions, ownership confusion, and weak invalidation boundaries between cooperating processes.
defensive_value: Helps explain bugs that appear only when multiple trusted components disagree about current state.

name: Adversarial Test Theme and Scenario Mapping
description: Translate real-world offensive technique families into safe internal test themes for authorized assessment, such as client-side trust abuse, parser confusion, insecure updater trust, sandbox boundary review, or hidden admin-path discovery. Keep the focus on what should be tested and observed, not on weaponization detail.
defensive_value: Lets defenders plan realistic adversarial assessments against their own software without needing exploit playbooks.

name: Import, Export, and Syscall Boundary Mapping
description: Map imported libraries, exported interfaces, privileged OS interactions, and sensitive system-call or API families used by the application. This helps defenders understand which external trust boundaries the binary actually depends on at runtime.
defensive_value: Highlights the real operating-system and library trust boundaries that deserve deeper review and monitoring.

name: Resource, Manifest, and Embedded Policy Extraction
description: Extract and review manifests, bundled resources, entitlement declarations, embedded policies, localization tables, and installer metadata that shape runtime behavior. These artifacts often reveal hidden capabilities, permissions, and fallback logic not obvious from the main code path.
defensive_value: Surfaces security-relevant behavior encoded outside the primary source logic.

name: Decompiler Confidence and Unknown-Code Triage
description: Track which functions decompile cleanly, which remain ambiguous, and which regions require manual review due to indirect control flow, custom allocators, obfuscation, or unusual calling conventions. Use this to prioritize analyst time instead of treating the whole binary as equally opaque.
defensive_value: Improves reverse-engineering efficiency and reduces the chance that risky code is ignored because it is harder to read.

name: Release-to-Release Binary Diff Prioritisation
description: Compare adjacent builds or versions to locate security-relevant deltas in binaries, resources, runtime guards, and embedded policy. Focus on changes that affect trust enforcement, parser behavior, update flow, permissions, or exposure to untrusted content.
defensive_value: Turns release analysis into a structured review method for uncovering incomplete fixes or silent risk shifts.

name: Type, Structure, and Object-Lifecycle Reconstruction
description: Recover likely data structures, ownership rules, and object lifecycles from binaries or mixed-language components so reviewers can reason about state transitions and safety assumptions. Focus on where memory ownership, reference counts, and cleanup order influence trust or reliability.
defensive_value: Makes opaque native code more reviewable and reveals where lifecycle confusion can create high-risk defects.

name: Message Schema and Serialization Map Recovery
description: Recover message layouts, field semantics, serialization schemas, and version markers used in local IPC, caches, sync traffic, or companion protocols. Understanding the schema boundary is often more valuable for defense than only understanding the transport.
defensive_value: Helps defenders reason about malformed-state handling, compatibility drift, and input validation boundaries.

name: State-Machine and Error-Code Reconstruction
description: Reconstruct major application states, mode transitions, and error outcomes by correlating code, strings, resources, and runtime traces. This is useful where security behavior depends on specific internal modes rather than simple on or off conditions.
defensive_value: Makes hidden operational states explicit so test plans can target the riskiest lifecycle edges.

name: Runtime Trace and Behavioral Timeline Capture
description: Capture high-level runtime behavior across process startup, login, update, import, sync, and shutdown phases to understand which code paths and trust decisions actually execute under real workflows. Use traces to validate or correct assumptions made during static analysis.
defensive_value: Bridges the gap between static review and real execution so analysts target the paths that matter most.

name: API Hooking and Sensitive Operation Observation
description: Instrument sensitive runtime operations such as file writes, crypto use, update validation, privilege changes, IPC calls, and network requests to understand the application's real security behavior. The goal is observation and coverage, not bypass.
defensive_value: Makes hidden runtime trust decisions observable so defensive controls and tests can be aligned to them.

name: Filesystem, Registry, and Config Mutation Observation
description: Observe what state the application creates, modifies, or trusts during normal and edge-case workflows, including configuration stores, registry keys, caches, temp files, and policy overlays. Focus on mutations that affect trust, recovery, or persistence.
defensive_value: Helps identify hidden persistence or trust-changing behavior that static inspection may miss.

name: Harness and Coverage Readiness Mapping
description: Identify which components are structurally suitable for isolated harnessing, deterministic replay, or coverage-guided robustness testing. Focus on decoupling inputs from side effects and on determining what environment is needed to exercise risky code safely.
defensive_value: Improves the practical quality of internal testing by identifying where deeper robustness work is actually feasible.

name: Fault Injection and Negative-State Observation
description: Trigger controlled error conditions such as missing files, denied permissions, malformed metadata, expired tokens, and partial network failure to observe how the application fails. Defensive review should focus on whether failure modes preserve trust and evidence quality.
defensive_value: Finds failure-handling weaknesses that only appear when the software is stressed or partially degraded.

name: License, Entitlement, and Feature Toggle Flow Tracing
description: Trace how feature availability, licensing, entitlements, and experimental flags are evaluated across the stack. These product controls often double as trust decisions, especially when they gate admin-only or high-risk functionality.
defensive_value: Separates true authorization from cosmetic gating and helps target review at the most security-relevant decision points.

name: Emulator and Virtualised Testbed Readiness Review
description: Assess whether a software or firmware target can be observed safely in emulation, virtual machines, or synthetic device environments, and which trust assumptions break there. This is useful for defensive repeatability, regression testing, and non-destructive analysis.
defensive_value: Improves repeatable testing for opaque targets without requiring direct production-device analysis every time.

name: Cross-Platform Wrapper and Shared-Core Divergence Review
description: Compare Windows, macOS, Linux, mobile, or appliance wrappers around a shared core to identify where platform-specific glue weakens otherwise consistent security behavior. Divergence in helpers, packaging, sandboxing, or update logic often creates platform-only risk.
defensive_value: Prevents review programs from missing vulnerabilities that only exist in one platform wrapper around a common product.

name: Crash Artifact, Symbol, and Build Provenance Correlation
description: Correlate crash outputs, symbol availability, build metadata, compiler flags, and release provenance so analysts can trace reliability or trust issues back to concrete build conditions. This is especially useful in large products with many branches or partial symbol coverage.
defensive_value: Improves root-cause quality and helps security teams separate product bugs from build-process or deployment mistakes.

name: Broker Contract and IPC Grammar Reconstruction
description: Recover the contract between low-trust clients and privileged brokers by mapping message grammar, command families, required fields, and rejection conditions. This makes it possible to review broker trust assumptions as a formal interface rather than as scattered code snippets.
defensive_value: Turns privileged IPC into a structured review artifact and highlights missing validation or overbroad broker capabilities.
